lesson.learn: The universe is home to numerous small bodies, such as asteroids and comets, that can pose a threat to life on Earth. These cosmic objects have been impacting our planet for billions of years, shaping its geology and potentially influencing the course of evolution. Understanding these events is crucial for mitigating future threats and ensuring human survival. By studying near-Earth objects (NEOs), scientists aim to identify potential risks and develop strategies for prevention.
lesson.simpler: Imagine Earth as a small ball in a big pool table, with asteroids and comets being the balls that could crash into it at any moment. These space rocks have been around since the beginning of our solar system and are still floating around us today. Sometimes they get too close to Earth and can cause damage.
lesson.keyTakeaway: The most important insight is that Earth is constantly exposed to potential threats from cosmic objects, and scientists must be vigilant in monitoring their orbits to prevent catastrophic collisions.
lesson.deeperDive: Recent research suggests that a significant portion of the solar system's mass is comprised of small bodies, including asteroids and comets. These objects are not just random debris; they have been perturbed into chaotic orbits due to gravitational interactions with other planets. In fact, it is estimated that up to 100 million objects larger than 1 km in diameter exist in our solar system. The largest known asteroid, Ceres, is now classified as a dwarf planet and serves as an example of these massive objects. NASA's Near-Earth Object Program has been tracking thousands of NEOs since its inception in the 1990s.
lesson.funFact: A notable example of a cosmic collision is the Chicxulub asteroid impact that occurred around 66 million years ago, contributing to the extinction of the dinosaurs. This event was so significant that it left an indelible mark on our planet's geology and atmosphere.

people: * Dr. Brian Cox - astrophysicist who has worked extensively with NASA
* Carolyn Porco - planetary scientist who led the team studying Saturn's moon, Titan
* Dr. Neil deGrasse Tyson - astrophysicist and science communicator
places: * NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL) - California, USA
years: * 2004 - the year a massive asteroid, Shoemaker-Levy 9, was discovered entering Jupiter's orbit
* 2013 - the year NASA announced its plans to capture and redirect an asteroid for further study
* 2020 - the year a large asteroid was spotted on a potential collision course with Earth
